payload.context: Audience: global Korean learners, K-culture fans, travelers preparing for Korea, and self-study beginners/intermediates. Product should reduce overwhelm and guide one short learning loop.

jtbd_output.core_job: When I want to learn Korean but find traditional textbooks boring, I want to practice through real K-culture locations and stories I'm emotionally invested in, so I can build language skills that feel connected to the culture I actually care about.

outcomes.outcome: Retain vocabulary in meaningful context (tied to places, shows, music I follow)
Feel motivated to practice daily without app fatigue or guilt
Know where/how to use Korean in real settings (visiting Korea, talking to fans online)
Experience progress that's visible and celebrates small wins
Share learning progress with Korean fan community or friends

context.when: During downtime (commute, lunch break, evening wind-down); triggered by K-culture consumption (watching shows, music drops, planning Korea trip)
context.where: Mobile, anywhere; ideally places connected to learning (coffee shops near Korean restaurants, travel planning)
context.frequency: Daily habit desired (5-15 min sessions); seasonal spikes (before Korea trip, new K-drama release)

constraints: Attention span limited to micro-sessions; cannot demand 30+ min commitment
Audio pronunciation feedback requires native speaker quality or learner gets discouraged
K-culture location data must be accurate (wrong place matches break immersion/utility)
Learner likely has low-intermediate Korean level; vocabulary must scaffold progressively
Competing with high-dopamine K-culture content (TikTok, streaming); must feel like entertainment, not homework
Cultural sensitivity: content must avoid stereotyping or performative representation

competing_solutions.weakness: Generic contexts; no K-culture connection; Korean content thin; learner feels disconnected from motivation

competing_solutions.solution: K-drama / K-music with subtitles (consumption without instruction)
competing_solutions.weakness: Passive learning; vocabulary acquired unsystematically; no structured path; frustration when can't understand

competing_solutions.solution: Korean language tutors / classes (Italki, university courses)
competing_solutions.weakness: Expensive; time-locked; formal tone mismatches casual K-culture interest; requires commitment learner may not sustain

competing_solutions.solution: Non-consumption: learner just watches K-content in English/native language, gives up on learning Korean
competing_solutions.weakness: Misses deeper connection; regret; feels excluded from Korean-speaking fan spaces

payload.name: Maya Chen

demographics.location: Los Angeles, CA (or major US metro with K-culture hub)
demographics.family_status: Single; close friend group active in K-pop/K-drama fandom

goals.goal: Build vocabulary recognition tied to K-drama locations, K-pop lyrics, and real-world Korean settings so words stick without rote memorization
goals.motivation: Generic textbook vocabulary feels disconnected; emotional investment in K-culture makes learning feel like fandom engagement, not homework

goals.goal: Establish daily 5–15 minute Korean practice habit that feels effortless and rewarding, not obligatory or guilt-inducing
goals.motivation: Competing with high-dopamine TikTok/streaming content; needs microlearning that delivers immediate satisfaction to sustain habit

goals.goal: Gain practical ability to use Korean in real contexts: ordering at Korean restaurants, understanding fan interactions online, conversing during Korea trip
goals.motivation: Passive consumption leaves her excluded from Korean-speaking fan spaces; wants agency and authentic connection to culture

goals.goal: See visible progress and celebrate incremental wins (vocabulary milestones, phrase mastery, location unlocks)
goals.motivation: Dopamine reward loop essential to compete with entertainment alternatives; small wins sustain motivation across weeks

goals.goal: Share learning milestones with K-culture fan friends or online communities to deepen social bonds and accountability
goals.motivation: Social proof and peer encouragement strengthen habit; fandom is identity; learning Korean elevates status within community

pain_points.pain: Duolingo/Babbel feel generic and disconnected from K-culture passion; motivation evaporates after 2–3 weeks because context is irrelevant
pain_points.current_workaround: Cycles between app abandonment and guilt-driven re-downloads; relies on K-drama subtitles to passively absorb vocabulary

pain_points.pain: Watching K-dramas with subtitles is emotionally engaging but learning is unsystematic; frustration when can't parse dialogue even with context clues
pain_points.current_workaround: Googles random phrases mid-show; gives up on understanding; feels excluded from Korean fan discourse

pain_points.pain: Audio pronunciation feedback from generic apps sounds robotic or unhelpful; if feedback quality is poor, confidence tanks and she stops practicing speaking
pain_points.current_workaround: Avoids speaking practice entirely; focuses only on reading/listening; misses speaking opportunity

pain_points.pain: Formal language classes (Italki, university) require 1–2 hour time blocks and expense ($15–30/session); misaligned with casual fandom learning goal
pain_points.current_workaround: Never commits; tells self she'll 'try tutoring eventually'; stays stuck in passive learning

pain_points.pain: Limited time (commute, lunch, wind-down only); cannot sustain 30+ minute sessions; apps demanding longer sessions feel like a chore
pain_points.current_workaround: Only engages in true downtime (5–10 min bursts); abandons anything requiring focus or planning

pain_points.pain: Inaccurate or irrelevant K-culture location data (wrong restaurant addresses, outdated K-drama filming sites) breaks immersion and utility
pain_points.current_workaround: Cross-references with Reddit/fan wikis; manually verifies locations; defeats purpose of integrated learning tool

pain_points.pain: Vocabulary progresses too slowly or feels unscaffolded; jumps from beginner to intermediate without clear path; gets lost
pain_points.current_workaround: Restarts apps multiple times; searches YouTube for 'Korean for beginners' series; inconsistent learning trajectory

pain_points.pain: App fatigue from notification pressure, streak mechanics, or performative gamification; feels manipulative rather than genuinely fun
pain_points.current_workaround: Mutes notifications; ignores streaks; uses app in isolation, losing social/community element

behaviors.behavior: Watches K-dramas 4–6 hours/week (evening wind-down, weekend binge); has favorite shows she rewatches; follows multiple series simultaneously
behaviors.context: Home, before bed, during lunch breaks at work

behaviors.behavior: Follows K-pop idols on TikTok, Instagram, YouTube; consumes short-form content 20–30 min/day; engages with fan communities (Reddit, Discord, Twitter)
behaviors.context: Mobile, everywhere; peak during new music releases or comeback seasons

behaviors.behavior: Uses Korean restaurant/travel planning as trigger for learning bursts; researches Korean dishes, reads fan-curated K-drama filming location guides before trips
behaviors.frequency: Seasonal spikes (before Korea trip, new K-drama release, fan meetup planning)

behaviors.behavior: Attempts language app (Duolingo, Babbel, HelloTalk) for 2–3 weeks, then stops; cycle repeats every 4–6 months when motivation resurfaces
behaviors.context: Mobile, during commute or lunch break

behaviors.behavior: Googles random Korean phrases from shows ('how to say X in Korean'); shares discoveries with friend group or fan community; seeks validation/explanation
Plans Korea trip (or daydreams about it); imagines ordering food, navigating, having conversations; feels anxiety about language barrier
behaviors.frequency: Recurring daydream, peaks when trip is scheduled

behaviors.behavior: Avoids speaking practice; prefers reading/listening; fears judgment or sounding wrong; no safe space to practice aloud
behaviors.context: When considering any speaking-focused tool or activity

payload.a_day_in_life: Maya wakes up, checks Instagram and TikTok while having coffee—her favorite K-pop group dropped a new behind-the-scenes video and she watches it twice, catching a few Korean words she wishes she understood. On her commute to the marketing office, she opens a language app she downloaded last week but scrolls past the generic 'greetings' lesson (boring); instead, she pulls up a K-drama she's rewatching and rewinds a scene three times trying to parse what a character just said, feeling frustrated she can't quite catch it. At lunch, a coworker mentions they're planning a Seoul trip in three months, and Maya's pulse quickens—she wants to go, wants to order food and chat with locals, but the thought of fumbling through Korean makes her anxious. She texts her friend group in their K-fandom Discord wondering if anyone knows how to say 'excuse me, what's the house special?' in Korean. One friend replies with a TikTok link to a language creator, and Maya bookmarks it, telling herself 'maybe this time' before scrolling back to episode five of her current K-drama, subtitles on, vocabulary slipping away.

path_suggestion.reason: 글로벌 추천 (아직 개인 기록 없음, weight=0.700)
